correctAnswer: VSD (ventricular septal defect) — most common congenital heart defect overall
quizQuestion: Which congenital heart defect is the most common overall (excluding bicuspid aortic valve), presenting with a holosystolic murmur loudest at the left lower sternal border, and resulting in a left-to-right shunt?
explanation: Ventricular septal defect (VSD) is the most common congenital heart defect excluding bicuspid aortic valve. Blood shunts left-to-right (high pressure LV to lower pressure RV) due to the pressure gradient. The murmur is holosystolic (pansystolic) and loudest at the left lower sternal border. Small VSDs (maladie de Roger) may close spontaneously. Large unrepaired VSDs cause progressive pulmonary hypertension and eventual Eisenmenger syndrome (shunt reversal to right-to-left, causing cyanosis).
visualDescription: Four-chamber cardiac diagram showing a defect in the interventricular septum with a left-to-right shunt arrow, and a stethoscope marker at the lower left sternal border
sourceName: Abbott Structural Heart — Types of Congenital Heart Defects

variants.quizQuestion: A large, unrepaired VSD causes progressive pulmonary hypertension and eventually reverses the shunt to right-to-left. What is this complication called?
variants.explanation: Eisenmenger syndrome occurs when a chronic large left-to-right shunt (VSD, ASD, PDA) causes pulmonary vascular remodelling and severe pulmonary hypertension. Eventually RV pressure exceeds LV pressure, reversing the shunt to right-to-left and producing cyanosis (late cyanosis). At this stage surgical repair is contraindicated; only lung transplantation can reverse the process.

correctAnswer: Ostium secundum ASD — most common type (~70%), defect in the fossa ovalis region of the atrial septum
quizQuestion: There are three main types of atrial septal defect. Which is the most common, accounting for roughly 70% of ASDs, and results from excessive reabsorption of the septum primum or failure of the septum secundum at the level of the fossa ovalis?
explanation: Ostium secundum ASD is the most common ASD (~70%), located in the central portion of the atrial septum (the fossa ovalis region). It results from excessive reabsorption of the septum primum or deficient growth of the septum secundum. Ostium primum ASD (~20%) is located inferiorly near the atrioventricular valves and is associated with Down syndrome and AV canal defects. Sinus venosus ASD (~10%) is located superiorly near the SVC-RA junction and is associated with anomalous pulmonary venous return.
visualDescription: Right atrial view of the interatrial septum showing three labeled defect positions: ostium secundum (central/fossa ovalis), ostium primum (inferior), and sinus venosus (superior near SVC)

variants.quizQuestion: Ostium primum ASD is associated with which chromosomal condition and which additional cardiac anomaly?
variants.correctAnswer: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) — associated with AV canal (endocardial cushion) defect
variants.explanation: Ostium primum ASD results from failure of the septum primum to fuse with the endocardial cushions and is part of the spectrum of AV septal/canal defects. It is strongly associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21). The complete AV canal defect also includes a VSD component and abnormal AV valves. Primum ASDs cannot be closed with a catheter-based device (unlike secundum) and require open cardiac surgery.

correctAnswer: Indomethacin (NSAID/COX inhibitor) — closes PDA by blocking prostaglandin synthesis
quizQuestion: In premature infants, the ductus arteriosus fails to close because elevated prostaglandin E2 keeps the smooth muscle relaxed. Which drug closes a patent ductus arteriosus by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis?
explanation: Indomethacin (a non-selective COX inhibitor) blocks prostaglandin E2 synthesis, allowing the ductus arteriosus to constrict and close. PGE2 relaxes ductal smooth muscle and maintains patency; removing it allows normal oxygen-induced closure. Ibuprofen (another NSAID) is also used with fewer renal side effects. Conversely, prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) is used to KEEP the ductus open in duct-dependent congenital heart defects (e.g., transposition of the great arteries, critical pulmonary stenosis) as a bridge to surgery.
visualDescription: Diagram of the neonatal heart showing the ductus arteriosus connecting the pulmonary artery to the aorta; one arrow showing PGE2 keeping it open, and an opposing arrow showing indomethacin blocking COX to close it

variants.quizQuestion: A newborn with transposition of the great arteries needs the ductus arteriosus kept open as a bridge to surgery. Which drug is given, and what is its mechanism?
variants.correctAnswer: Prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) — relaxes ductal smooth muscle to maintain patency
variants.explanation: In duct-dependent congenital heart lesions, mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood (or pulmonary blood flow) depends on ductal patency. PGE1 (alprostadil) is infused to prevent spontaneous ductal closure by relaxing smooth muscle via cAMP. This buys time for surgical correction. Side effects include apnoea, fever, and hypotension, requiring monitoring and often intubation.

correctAnswer: Aortic stenosis — crescendo-decrescendo (diamond-shaped) systolic murmur, loudest at the right upper sternal border, radiating to the carotids
quizQuestion: Which valvular lesion produces a harsh crescendo-decrescendo (ejection) systolic murmur heard best at the right upper sternal border and radiating to the carotids, associated with a slow-rising carotid pulse (pulsus parvus et tardus)?
explanation: Aortic stenosis (AS) produces a midsystolic ejection murmur that crescendos then decrescendos ('diamond shaped') as blood is forced through the narrowed valve orifice. It is loudest at the right second intercostal space (aortic area) and radiates to the carotids. Associated findings: pulsus parvus et tardus (small, slow-rising carotid pulse), soft/absent A2, S4 gallop. Classical triad of severe AS: syncope, angina, heart failure. Prognosis worsens with symptom onset: angina = 5 years, syncope = 3 years, HF = 1–2 years.
visualDescription: Cardiac silhouette with aortic valve area labeled, murmur shape shown as a diamond/rhombus waveform timed with systole; carotid radiation arrow

correctAnswer: Mitral regurgitation — holosystolic (pansystolic) blowing murmur, loudest at the apex, radiating to the axilla
quizQuestion: Which valvular lesion produces a high-pitched, blowing holosystolic murmur that is loudest at the cardiac apex and radiates to the left axilla, starting with S1 and extending through to S2?
explanation: Mitral regurgitation (MR) produces a holosystolic (pansystolic) murmur that begins immediately with S1 and extends to or through A2 without a gap. It is high-pitched and blowing because blood flows retrograde from the high-pressure LV into the lower-pressure LA throughout systole. It is loudest at the apex and classically radiates to the axilla. Causes include rheumatic heart disease, mitral valve prolapse, papillary muscle dysfunction (post-MI), and infective endocarditis.
visualDescription: Phonocardiogram waveform showing a flat, rectangular murmur filling the entire systole from S1 to S2, with a stethoscope icon placed at the cardiac apex

correctAnswer: Mitral valve prolapse — mid-systolic click followed by a late systolic murmur; most common valvular abnormality
quizQuestion: This is the most common valvular heart disease, more prevalent in young women, and is characterised on auscultation by a mid-systolic click at the apex. Name the condition and explain what causes the click.
explanation: Mitral valve prolapse (MVP) is the most common valvular abnormality. The mid-systolic click is caused by sudden tensing of the chordae tendineae as the redundant, myxomatous mitral leaflets prolapse backward into the LA at mid-systole. The click is followed by a late systolic murmur (due to mild MR from incomplete leaflet coaptation). The click moves earlier with manoeuvres that decrease LV volume (standing, Valsalva) and later with increased LV volume (squatting). MVP is associated with Marfan syndrome.
visualDescription: Phonocardiogram showing S1, then a distinct sharp click in mid-systole, followed by a short high-pitched murmur extending to S2, with a stethoscope icon at the apex

variants.quizQuestion: Standing up quickly (or the Valsalva manoeuvre) causes the mid-systolic click of MVP to move earlier in systole. Why?
variants.correctAnswer: Decreased LV volume causes the leaflets to prolapse sooner in systole
variants.explanation: Decreased preload (standing, Valsalva strain phase) reduces LV volume, so the mitral leaflets reach their prolapse point earlier in systole — the click moves toward S1 and the murmur lengthens. Increased preload (squatting, leg raise) increases LV volume, delays prolapse — click moves later, murmur shortens. This dynamic auscultation pattern is a hallmark of MVP and a favourite USMLE manoeuvre question.

correctAnswer: LAD (left anterior descending artery) — anterior LV wall and anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum
quizQuestion: Which coronary artery is called the 'widow maker' because its occlusion causes an anterior STEMI affecting the largest territory of the left ventricle — the anterior wall and anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum?
explanation: The left anterior descending (LAD) artery, a branch of the left main coronary artery, supplies the anterior wall of the left ventricle, the anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum, the apex, and the bundle branches (left bundle branch block can result from proximal LAD occlusion). It runs in the anterior interventricular groove. Occlusion causes anterior STEMI, the most common and most lethal STEMI. ST elevation in V1–V4 on ECG.
visualDescription: Anterior view of the heart: LAD highlighted in the anterior interventricular groove with shaded territory covering the anterior LV wall and anterior septum; 'widow maker' label
sourceName: StatPearls — Anatomy, Thorax, Heart Coronary Arteries
distractors: Left main coronary artery (no direct territory)

variants.quizQuestion: Which coronary artery supplies the lateral wall of the left ventricle, and which ECG leads show ST changes with its occlusion?
variants.correctAnswer: Left circumflex (LCx) artery — ST changes in leads I, aVL, V5, V6
variants.explanation: The left circumflex (LCx) artery travels in the atrioventricular groove and supplies the lateral wall of the LV. Occlusion causes a lateral STEMI with ST elevation in leads I, aVL (high lateral) and V5, V6 (low lateral). In left-dominant circulation, the LCx also supplies the inferior wall. The LCx gives off the obtuse marginal branches.

correctAnswer: RCA (right coronary artery) — inferior LV wall, RV, SA node, and AV node
quizQuestion: Which coronary artery supplies the inferior wall of the LV, the right ventricle, the SA node (in 60% of individuals), and the AV node (in 80–90%), making its occlusion prone to cause bradyarrhythmias?
explanation: The right coronary artery (RCA) travels in the right atrioventricular groove. It gives off the SA nodal artery (in ~60% of individuals, the rest from LCx) and the AV nodal artery (in ~80–90%). It gives off the acute marginal branch to the RV free wall and, in right-dominant circulations (~85% of people), the posterior descending artery (PDA) supplying the inferior LV wall and posterior septum. Inferior STEMI (ST elevation in II, III, aVF) from RCA occlusion is frequently complicated by sinus bradycardia, AV block, and RV infarction.
visualDescription: Right lateral view of the heart: RCA coursing in the right AV groove, SA nodal and AV nodal branches labeled, acute marginal branch to RV, and PDA labeled at the inferior interventricular groove
sourceName: StatPearls — Anatomy, Thorax, Heart Coronary Arteries
distractors: LAD — inferior wall and SA/AV nodes
LCx — inferior wall and SA node in all

correctAnswer: SA node action potential — Phase 0 (Ca²⁺ current), Phase 3 (K⁺ efflux), Phase 4 (funny current If via slow Na⁺ influx)
quizQuestion: The SA node action potential has no stable resting membrane potential and automatically fires. What is the ionic basis of the spontaneous Phase 4 depolarisation (pacemaker potential) in SA nodal cells?
explanation: The SA node pacemaker potential (Phase 4) is driven by: (1) the 'funny current' (If) — inward Na⁺ current through HCN channels that opens on hyperpolarisation; (2) decreasing K⁺ conductance (less IK outward current as time passes); (3) a slow inward Ca²⁺ current (T-type Ca²⁺ channels at ~ -55 mV). These together cause slow, spontaneous depolarisation from ~ -60 mV to threshold (~-40 mV). Ivabradine selectively blocks If channels to slow heart rate without affecting contractility. Unlike ventricular AP, the SA node Phase 0 depolarisation is carried by L-type Ca²⁺ channels (blocked by verapamil/diltiazem), not fast Na⁺ channels.
visualDescription: SA node action potential graph: smooth, spontaneous upslope from -60 mV to -40 mV threshold (Phase 4 labeled with If arrow), rapid Phase 0 upstroke via Ca²⁺ channels, Phase 3 repolarisation via K⁺
sourceName: CV Physiology — Sinoatrial Node Action Potentials
distractors: Only K⁺ efflux drives pacemaker potential
HCN channels are blocked in SA node

variants.quizQuestion: Ivabradine slows heart rate in heart failure and stable angina without affecting contractility. Which channel does it block?
variants.correctAnswer: HCN channels (funny current, If) in the SA node
variants.explanation: Ivabradine selectively blocks HCN channels carrying the If current in the SA node, slowing Phase 4 depolarisation and reducing heart rate. Unlike beta-blockers, it has no negative inotropic effect, making it useful in patients with HFrEF who need rate control but cannot tolerate beta-blockade. It is contraindicated if baseline HR < 60 bpm.

correctAnswer: Ventricular action potential — Phase 0 (rapid Na⁺ influx), Phase 1 (K⁺ efflux), Phase 2 (plateau, Ca²⁺ in), Phase 3 (K⁺ repolarisation), Phase 4 (stable resting ~-90 mV)
quizQuestion: The ventricular cardiac action potential has five phases (0–4). Which ion channel is responsible for the rapid Phase 0 upstroke, and what causes the characteristic Phase 2 plateau — unique to cardiac muscle compared to skeletal or nerve AP?
explanation: Phase 0: rapid depolarisation by fast voltage-gated Na⁺ channels (blocked by Class I antiarrhythmics). Phase 1: partial repolarisation by transient K⁺ efflux (Ito). Phase 2: plateau — L-type Ca²⁺ channels (blocked by Class IV — verapamil, diltiazem) balance K⁺ efflux; triggers Ca²⁺-induced Ca²⁺ release (CICR) from the SR for contraction. Phase 3: K⁺ channels (IKr, IKs) repolarise the membrane. Phase 4: stable resting at ~ -90 mV (maintained by IK1). The plateau is unique to cardiac muscle and accounts for the long refractory period preventing tetanic contraction.
visualDescription: Ventricular AP graph: sharp Phase 0 upstroke (Na⁺) to +20 mV, brief Phase 1 notch, long Phase 2 plateau at 0 mV (Ca²⁺ labeled), Phase 3 rapid repolarisation (K⁺), Phase 4 stable at -90 mV
distractors: Phase 0 via L-type Ca²⁺, Phase 2 via Na⁺
Phase 0 via K⁺ channels, Phase 2 via Cl⁻
Phase 0 via slow Na⁺, Phase 2 via K⁺ plateau
Phase 0 via T-type Ca²⁺, Phase 2 via Na⁺
All phases via non-specific cation channels

correctAnswer: Systolic heart failure (HFrEF) — EF ≤ 40%, impaired contractility; treated with ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics
quizQuestion: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) is defined by an EF ≤ 40% and is caused by impaired myocardial contractility. What is the most common cause, and which drug class improves survival by reducing afterload and preventing cardiac remodelling?
explanation: HFrEF is caused most commonly by ischaemic heart disease (post-MI) and dilated cardiomyopathy. The LV dilates and contractility fails, reducing stroke volume and ejection fraction. Neurohormonal activation (RAAS, sympathetic) initially compensates but accelerates remodelling. ACE inhibitors (or ARBs) reduce afterload and preload and prevent cardiac remodelling — they are the cornerstone of HFrEF therapy and reduce mortality. Beta-blockers (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol) also reduce mortality. Loop diuretics improve symptoms by reducing preload. SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) are newer mortality-reducing agents.
visualDescription: Side-by-side diagram of normal LV (round, contracting to ~60% EF) vs dilated HFrEF LV (enlarged, thin walls, poor contraction to ~30% EF); treatment pill icons for ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker
distractors: EF ≤ 40%, treated with calcium channel blockers as first-line

variants.quizQuestion: Heart failure with preserved EF (HFpEF) is defined by EF ≥ 50% and is caused by what underlying mechanism?
variants.correctAnswer: Diastolic dysfunction — impaired LV relaxation and filling due to a stiff ventricle
variants.explanation: HFpEF (diastolic heart failure) results from impaired LV relaxation and reduced compliance, typically from hypertension (most common cause), hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, or restrictive cardiomyopathy. The LV cannot fill properly, raising LVEDP and causing pulmonary congestion despite a normal ejection fraction. Unlike HFrEF, ACE inhibitors and beta-blockers have not shown mortality benefit in HFpEF; management focuses on diuretics for symptom relief and treating underlying hypertension.

correctAnswer: Preload — ventricular end-diastolic volume/pressure (LVEDP); increased by venous return
quizQuestion: In cardiac physiology, 'preload' is the stretch on the ventricular walls just before contraction. Clinically, what is the best bedside measure of preload, and what is the physiological equivalent?
explanation: Preload is the ventricular wall tension at end-diastole, proportional to the end-diastolic volume (EDV) and approximated by the left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP). It follows Starling's law: increased preload → increased stretch → increased force of contraction (within limits). LVEDP is elevated in fluid overload, heart failure, and MR. Clinical correlates: JVP and PCWP measure preload. Diuretics and nitrates reduce preload (venodilation). Afterload is the resistance against which the LV must contract, clinically approximated by systemic vascular resistance (SVR).
visualDescription: Frank-Starling curve graph: x-axis = preload (LVEDP/EDV), y-axis = stroke volume; upsloping normal curve, flat/depressed curve for heart failure, with interventions (diuretics reduce preload, inotropes shift curve up)

correctAnswer: Cardiogenic shock — pump failure; elevated PCWP, low CO, high SVR; treat with inotropes ± mechanical support
quizQuestion: Which type of shock results from primary cardiac failure (e.g., massive MI), is characterised by high pulmonary capillary wedge pressure, low cardiac output, and high SVR, and presents with cold, clammy skin and pulmonary oedema?
explanation: Cardiogenic shock results from impaired cardiac contractility (massive MI, acute decompensated HF, myocarditis, severe valvular disease). The failing pump cannot maintain forward flow: CO↓, LVEDP↑ (PCWP↑, causing pulmonary oedema), and SVR↑ (compensatory vasoconstriction causing cold, clammy skin). Management: inotropes (dobutamine), vasopressors (norepinephrine if hypotensive), and mechanical circulatory support (IABP, Impella, ECMO). Revascularisation (PCI) is the definitive treatment in STEMI-related cardiogenic shock.
visualDescription: Hemodynamic profile table comparing cardiogenic shock (CO↓, PCWP↑, SVR↑) with hypovolemic (CO↓, PCWP↓, SVR↑) and distributive/septic (CO↑, PCWP↓, SVR↓) shock

variants.quizQuestion: Distributive shock (septic, anaphylactic, neurogenic) is characterised by what haemodynamic pattern — specifically CO, SVR, and skin temperature?
variants.correctAnswer: High CO, low SVR, warm skin (vasodilation) — except neurogenic which has bradycardia
variants.explanation: Distributive shock involves pathological vasodilation reducing SVR. The heart compensates by increasing CO (hyperdynamic phase in early sepsis). SVR is low, and skin is warm (pink, flushed) due to cutaneous vasodilation. This contrasts with cardiogenic and hypovolemic shock where vasoconstriction makes skin cold and clammy. Neurogenic shock (SCI) additionally has bradycardia due to loss of sympathetic tone (no compensatory tachycardia).

correctAnswer: Hypovolemic shock — decreased preload (PCWP↓), compensatory high SVR and tachycardia; treat with IV fluids
quizQuestion: A trauma patient loses 2 litres of blood. Which type of shock results, what are the haemodynamic parameters (CO, SVR, PCWP), and what is the initial treatment?
explanation: Haemorrhagic (hypovolemic) shock: blood loss reduces venous return and preload (PCWP↓), decreasing stroke volume and CO↓. Compensatory mechanisms increase HR and SVR (vasoconstriction → cold, clammy skin). The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system and ADH activate to restore volume. Initial treatment is 1–2 L IV crystalloid (normal saline or Ringer's lactate), followed by blood products (1:1:1 packed RBCs:FFP:platelets for massive haemorrhage). Definitive treatment is haemostasis.
visualDescription: Trauma patient silhouette with blood loss arrow, haemodynamic panel showing CO↓, SVR↑, PCWP↓; treatment icons: IV line with crystalloid and blood product bags

correctAnswer: Thoracic aortic aneurysm — associated with Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutation) and tertiary syphilis (obliterative endarteritis of vasa vasorum)
quizQuestion: Which type of aortic aneurysm is associated with Marfan syndrome (ascending aorta, cystic medial necrosis) and with tertiary syphilis (obliterative endarteritis of the vasa vasorum)?
explanation: Thoracic aortic aneurysms affect the ascending or descending thoracic aorta. Marfan syndrome (autosomal dominant, FBN1 mutation on chr.15 — fibrillin-1 deficiency) causes cystic medial necrosis and weakness of the aortic wall, leading to ascending aortic aneurysm and aortic root dilation (risk of dissection and AR). Tertiary syphilis causes obliterative endarteritis of the vasa vasorum, ischaemic necrosis of the aortic media, and 'tree-bark' ascending aortic aneurysm. Modern causes: hypertension and bicuspid aortic valve (in the ascending aorta).
visualDescription: Sagittal chest diagram: dilated ascending aorta labeled 'thoracic aneurysm'; two aetiological icons — Marfan body habitus silhouette and syphilis spirochete
sourceName: NCBI Bookshelf — Aneurysm and Dissection of the Aorta
distractors: Thoracic — associated with Ehlers-Danlos type IV only
Thoracic — located below the renal arteries

correctAnswer: Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) — atherosclerosis, most commonly infrarenal; risk: male, smoker, >65 years
quizQuestion: Which type of aortic aneurysm is most common overall, affects the infrarenal aorta most frequently, is caused primarily by atherosclerosis, and is screened for by ultrasound in men aged 65–75 who have ever smoked?
explanation: Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) is most common at the infrarenal aorta (below the renal arteries) because the abdominal aorta has fewer vasa vasorum and is more susceptible to atherosclerosis. Risk factors: male sex (~5:1), age >65, smoking (most modifiable RF), family history, hypertension. Diagnosis and surveillance: ultrasound. Repair threshold: >5.5 cm in men, >5 cm in women, or rapidly expanding. Rupture presents with the classic triad: abdominal/back pain, pulsatile abdominal mass, and hypotension.
visualDescription: Abdominal anatomy diagram: aorta shown with dilated segment labeled infrarenal AAA (below the renal artery takeoffs), with ultrasound probe icon and risk factor label (smoking, male, age 65+)
distractors: Screened by CT in women only

variants.quizQuestion: A patient presents with sudden severe back and abdominal pain, a pulsatile abdominal mass, and haemorrhagic shock. What is the diagnosis and immediate management?
variants.correctAnswer: Ruptured AAA — emergent surgical repair (open or endovascular)
variants.explanation: Ruptured AAA is a surgical emergency with mortality > 80% without immediate repair. The classic triad is sudden abdominal/back pain, pulsatile abdominal mass, and haemodynamic collapse. Do not delay for CT if clinical diagnosis is obvious. Emergent open surgery or EVAR (endovascular aneurysm repair) is required. Permissive hypotension strategy (target SBP ~90 mmHg) prevents further dissection while awaiting theatre.

correctAnswer: Virchow's triad — (1) venous stasis, (2) hypercoagulability, (3) endothelial injury
quizQuestion: Named for the 19th-century German physician who described the pathogenesis of venous thrombosis, what are the three factors in the classic triad predisposing to thrombus formation?
explanation: Virchow's triad (described 1856): (1) Venous stasis — slow blood flow (immobility, long-haul flights, heart failure, post-operative); (2) Hypercoagulability — inherited (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, protein C/S deficiency, antithrombin III deficiency) or acquired (pregnancy, OCP, malignancy, antiphospholipid syndrome); (3) Endothelial/intimal injury — trauma, surgery, inflammation, atherosclerosis. Presence of two or more factors markedly increases DVT/PE risk. Factor V Leiden is the most common inherited thrombophilia.
visualDescription: Triangle diagram labeled 'Virchow's Triad' with three vertices: 'Venous Stasis,' 'Hypercoagulability,' and 'Endothelial Injury'; examples listed at each vertex

correctAnswer: Femoral and popliteal veins — most common DVT locations; high PE risk from proximal lower limb DVT
quizQuestion: Deep vein thrombosis most commonly affects the deep veins of the proximal lower extremity. Which two specific veins are the most clinically important sites, and why do proximal DVTs carry higher PE risk than calf DVTs?
explanation: The femoral and popliteal veins are the most clinically important DVT sites. Proximal lower limb DVT (femoral/popliteal) carries significantly higher pulmonary embolism risk than isolated distal (calf) DVT because the larger clot burden can more easily propagate and embolise to the pulmonary vasculature. Isolated distal DVT may be observed or treated depending on symptoms. Proximal DVT requires anticoagulation. Calf DVT PE risk is low (~1–2%) without propagation. Diagnosis: compression ultrasound (first-line), D-dimer for exclusion in low probability.
visualDescription: Lower extremity venous anatomy: deep veins labeled from ankle (posterior tibial, peroneal) to knee (popliteal) to thigh (femoral/superficial femoral, profunda femoris, common femoral); proximal segment shaded to indicate high PE risk
distractors: Popliteal vein only — calf DVT safe
Iliac veins — not classified as DVT
Femoral vein — no PE risk if distal to inguinal ligament

correctAnswer: Peripheral arterial disease (PAD) — atherosclerosis → intermittent claudication → rest pain → critical limb ischaemia
quizQuestion: Atherosclerosis of the peripheral arteries produces ischaemic symptoms in the lower limbs. What is the classic symptom of claudication, and what progression of symptoms indicates worsening from moderate to critical limb ischaemia?
explanation: Peripheral arterial disease (PAD): atherosclerosis narrows limb arteries. Claudication is reproducible muscle pain/cramping on exertion, relieved by rest (analogous to angina). As disease progresses: rest pain (constant ischaemic pain at rest, especially at night/elevation — patient hangs legs over bedside for relief), then critical limb ischaemia (CLI) with tissue loss — non-healing ulcers, gangrene. The '6 Ps' of acute limb ischaemia: Pain, Pulselessness, Pallor, Paraesthesia, Paralysis, Poikilothermia. Ankle-brachial index (ABI) < 0.9 is diagnostic of PAD; < 0.4 = critical ischaemia.
visualDescription: Progression chart from normal limb to claudication to rest pain to critical limb ischaemia, with ABI values (0.9 to <0.4) and a foreground image of an ischaemic foot with ulcer
distractors: DVT — worsens with dependency, improved by elevation
Venous claudication — not relieved by rest at all
Neurogenic claudication — bilateral, worsens with walking uphill
Peripheral neuropathy — burning, no reproducible pattern
Spinal stenosis — relieved by sitting/bending forward

variants.quizQuestion: The ankle-brachial index (ABI) is used to diagnose peripheral arterial disease. What ABI value is considered normal, and what value indicates critical limb ischaemia?
variants.correctAnswer: Normal ABI ≥ 0.9; critical limb ischaemia ABI < 0.4
variants.explanation: ABI = (ankle systolic BP) ÷ (brachial systolic BP). Normal ≥ 0.9; 0.7–0.9 = mild PAD; 0.4–0.7 = moderate PAD (claudication); < 0.4 = severe/critical limb ischaemia (rest pain, ulcers, gangrene). ABI > 1.3 suggests calcified, non-compressible vessels (common in diabetics), which is also abnormal. ABI is a simple, non-invasive, reproducible bedside test.

correctAnswer: Sinus venosus ASD — located near the SVC-RA junction; associated with partial anomalous pulmonary venous return
quizQuestion: The least common of the three main ASD types, this defect is located superiorly near the SVC–right atrium junction and is specifically associated with partial anomalous pulmonary venous return. Name it.
explanation: Sinus venosus ASD accounts for ~10% of ASDs and is located at the junction of the SVC (or rarely IVC) with the right atrium, outside the true atrial septum. It is uniquely associated with partial anomalous pulmonary venous return (PAPVR), where one or more right pulmonary veins drain anomalously into the SVC or RA rather than the LA. This causes a left-to-right shunt. Unlike ostium secundum ASD, sinus venosus ASD cannot be closed by catheter-based device and requires open surgical repair with a pericardial patch to re-route pulmonary venous return.
visualDescription: Right atrial view of the interatrial septum: sinus venosus defect positioned near the SVC orifice at the top, with an anomalous right pulmonary vein draining into the SVC labeled

correctAnswer: Neurogenic shock — vasodilation + bradycardia (loss of sympathetic tone); treat with vasopressors (norepinephrine) and IV fluids
quizQuestion: A patient with T5 spinal cord injury develops hypotension and an unexpectedly SLOW heart rate (bradycardia). Which type of distributive shock is this, and why does bradycardia occur instead of the expected compensatory tachycardia?
explanation: Neurogenic shock results from high spinal cord injury (above T6), causing loss of descending sympathetic tone. Vasodilation (low SVR) reduces preload and BP. Crucially, the compensatory sympathetic tachycardia response is also ablated, leaving unopposed parasympathetic (vagal) tone — causing paradoxical bradycardia rather than tachycardia. This distinguishes neurogenic from all other shock types (which cause reflex tachycardia). Treatment: IV fluids to restore preload, vasopressors (norepinephrine to restore SVR), and atropine or pacing for severe bradycardia.
visualDescription: Shock haemodynamic table: neurogenic shock highlighted with CO normal/↑, SVR↓↓, PCWP↓, HR↓ (bradycardia) — contrasting with septic shock (HR↑) in the same table
distractors: Septic shock — bradycardia in early phase
Obstructive shock — bradycardia due to tamponade

correctAnswer: Anaphylactic shock — IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation → massive histamine release → vasodilation + bronchospasm; treat with epinephrine IM
quizQuestion: A patient develops urticaria, angioedema, wheezing, and hypotension after a bee sting. What is the pathophysiological mechanism of this distributive shock, and what is the immediate first-line treatment?
explanation: Anaphylactic shock: IgE-mediated (Type I hypersensitivity) → mast cell and basophil degranulation → histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, prostaglandins → massive vasodilation (decreased SVR and preload), increased vascular permeability (angioedema, urticaria), bronchospasm, and laryngeal oedema. The haemodynamic pattern is distributive: high CO (initially), low SVR, tachycardia. First-line treatment: intramuscular epinephrine (adrenaline) 0.5 mg (1:1000) into the lateral thigh — acts as alpha-1 agonist (vasoconstriction) and beta-2 agonist (bronchodilation). IV fluids and corticosteroids are adjuncts.
visualDescription: Patient with urticarial rash and swollen lips (angioedema); haemodynamic panel showing distributive pattern; auto-injector (EpiPen) labeled as first-line treatment

correctAnswer: LAD → anterior MI; RCA → inferior MI (ST elevation II, III, aVF); LCx → lateral MI (I, aVL, V5, V6)
quizQuestion: On a 12-lead ECG, ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF localises the infarct to the inferior wall. Which coronary artery is most likely occluded, and which leads indicate LAD versus LCx territory?
explanation: ECG localisation of STEMI by territory: Anterior STEMI (V1–V4) = LAD occlusion (anterior LV + septum). Lateral STEMI (I, aVL, V5, V6) = LCx occlusion (lateral LV). Inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF) = RCA occlusion (~80%) or dominant LCx (~20%). Posterior STEMI (ST depression V1–V3 + upright T = posterior infarct; confirmed by posterior leads V7–V9) = RCA or LCx. RV infarction (right-sided leads V3R–V4R) complicates ~30% of inferior STEMIs — these patients should NOT receive nitrates (depend on RV preload).
visualDescription: 12-lead ECG schematic: leads coloured by territory — V1–V4 (LAD, anterior), I/aVL/V5–V6 (LCx, lateral), II/III/aVF (RCA, inferior); coronary artery diagram aligned below
sourceName: StatPearls — Anatomy, Thorax, Heart Coronary Arteries
distractors: ST elevation in V1–V4 = RCA territory

correctAnswer: Marfan syndrome — FBN1 mutation (fibrillin-1) on chromosome 15; ascending aortic aneurysm and aortic root dilation are the primary life-threatening manifestations
quizQuestion: Which autosomal dominant connective tissue disorder, caused by a mutation in the FBN1 gene encoding fibrillin-1 on chromosome 15, predisposes to ascending aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection, aortic regurgitation, and ectopia lentis?
explanation: Marfan syndrome (autosomal dominant, FBN1 gene, chr.15) causes cystic medial necrosis of the aorta due to defective fibrillin-1 in elastic tissue. The ascending aorta and aortic root dilate (aortic root diameter > 4 cm is the surgical threshold), causing aortic regurgitation and risk of Type A aortic dissection. Non-cardiac features include tall stature, long extremities (arachnodactyly), high arched palate, pectus deformities, and superior lens dislocation (ectopia lentis — up and out). Beta-blockers slow aortic root dilation; elective repair is offered at 5.0 cm.
visualDescription: Tall, thin patient silhouette with arachnodactyly, ascending aortic dilation diagram, and ectopia lentis eye inset; FBN1/chr.15 label
sourceName: PMC — Medical Treatment of Aortic Aneurysms in Marfan Syndrome

correctAnswer: Syphilitic (luetic) aortic aneurysm — tertiary syphilis, obliterative endarteritis of vasa vasorum → medial necrosis → ascending 'tree-bark' aneurysm
quizQuestion: In tertiary syphilis, Treponema pallidum causes obliterative endarteritis of the vasa vasorum supplying the aortic wall. What is the characteristic aneurysm that results, and how does the aortic intima appear grossly?
explanation: Tertiary syphilis (15–30 years after primary infection) causes obliterative endarteritis of the vasa vasorum — the small vessels nourishing the outer aortic wall. Ischaemic necrosis of the aortic media weakens the wall, leading to dilation — classically of the ascending aorta and aortic root. The intima develops a characteristic 'tree-bark' or wrinkled appearance. Complications include: aortic aneurysm (saccular or fusiform), aortic regurgitation (from aortic root dilation), and ostial coronary artery stenosis. Syphilitic aortitis is now rare due to antibiotic treatment.
visualDescription: Gross pathology photo simulation of the ascending aortic intima: irregular, wrinkled 'tree-bark' texture; ascending aorta outlined with saccular aneurysm at the root
sourceName: NCBI Bookshelf — Aneurysm and Dissection of the Aorta
distractors: Abdominal aorta most common in syphilis
Affects only the descending thoracic aorta
Obliterative endarteritis of coronary arteries only

correctAnswer: Factor V Leiden — most common inherited thrombophilia; activated protein C resistance; autosomal dominant
quizQuestion: Which inherited thrombophilia is the most common in the general population (~5% prevalence in Europeans), caused by a point mutation in Factor V that makes it resistant to cleavage by activated protein C (APC resistance)?
explanation: Factor V Leiden is a point mutation (Arg506Gln) in the Factor V gene, making Factor Va resistant to inactivation by activated protein C. APC normally cleaves and inactivates Factor Va and VIIIa to limit clot propagation. Resistance to APC leads to excess thrombin generation and hypercoagulability. It is the most common inherited thrombophilia (~5% prevalence in Europeans). Heterozygotes have 4–8x increased DVT risk; homozygotes have 80x increased risk. Associated with DVT, PE, and pregnancy-related thrombosis.
visualDescription: Coagulation cascade diagram: Factor V highlighted with a mutation marker at Arg506Gln; APC scissors shown unable to cleave the mutant Factor Va; DVT in the leg vein as the consequence

correctAnswer: Purkinje fibre AP — resembles ventricular AP but has automaticity (Phase 4 depolarisation) with intrinsic rate of ~40–60 bpm
quizQuestion: Purkinje fibres conduct action potentials rapidly to the ventricular myocardium. Unlike ordinary ventricular myocytes, they possess spontaneous automaticity. What is their intrinsic firing rate, and what happens to their automaticity when the SA node is functioning normally?
explanation: Purkinje fibres have the longest action potentials of any cardiac cell and possess intrinsic automaticity via the funny current (If), firing spontaneously at 40–60 bpm (slower than the SA node at 60–100 bpm and the AV node at 40–60 bpm). Under normal sinus rhythm, the SA node's faster rate overdrive suppresses the Purkinje fibres' automaticity — they are driven by the sinus impulse and do not fire spontaneously. If the SA node fails, Purkinje fibres can act as a 'subsidiary pacemaker' (ventricular escape rhythm at 40–60 bpm). Class I antiarrhythmics (Na⁺ channel blockers) preferentially affect Purkinje fibres.
visualDescription: Comparative AP waveform diagram: SA node (rapid Phase 4), AV node (intermediate), Purkinje/ventricular (long Phase 2 plateau, slow Phase 4), with intrinsic rates labeled for each
sourceName: Deranged Physiology — Ionic basis of spontaneous electrical activity
distractors: Purkinje rate 60–100 bpm (same as SA node)
Purkinje fibres fire faster than SA node

correctAnswer: PDA murmur — continuous 'machinery' murmur at the left upper sternal border (subclavicular), loudest at S2
quizQuestion: The patent ductus arteriosus connects the main pulmonary artery to the descending aorta. What is the characteristic murmur of a PDA, and why does it persist through both systole and diastole?
explanation: A PDA produces a continuous 'machinery' murmur because the pressure gradient between the aorta (high pressure) and pulmonary artery (low pressure) persists throughout both systole and diastole — so blood flows continuously from aorta to pulmonary artery across the ductus. The murmur is heard at the left upper sternal border/left infraclavicular area and is loudest at and around S2. Associated features: wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses, and active precordium from the increased pulmonary flow and LV volume overload.
visualDescription: Phonocardiogram showing a continuous murmur that crescendos into S2 and then decrescendos through diastole, resembling a 'machinery' sound; PDA anatomy diagram beside it
distractors: Diastolic only murmur at the apex
Holosystolic murmur at left lower sternal border

correctAnswer: ASD auscultation — fixed wide splitting of S2; soft systolic ejection murmur at left upper sternal border (increased pulmonary flow)
quizQuestion: Which congenital heart defect is classically identified by fixed, wide splitting of the second heart sound (S2) that does not vary with respiration, along with a soft systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border?
explanation: ASD causes fixed wide splitting of S2. Normally, inspiration increases RV filling and delays pulmonary valve closure (P2), causing physiological S2 splitting. In ASD, the left-to-right shunt delivers extra volume to the RV throughout the respiratory cycle, constantly delaying P2 — so S2 splitting is wide and does not change with respiration (fixed splitting). The systolic murmur is caused by increased flow across the pulmonary valve (functional pulmonary stenosis), not by flow through the ASD itself. A flow rumble at the tricuspid area (increased TR flow) may also be heard.
visualDescription: Phonocardiogram showing A2 and P2 widely and fixedly split in both inspiration and expiration, contrasted with normal (splits on inspiration only) and paradoxical splitting (splits on expiration)
distractors: VSD — holosystolic murmur, fixed S2 split
PDA — continuous murmur, no S2 split
Pulmonary stenosis — ejection click, wide split without right-to-left shunt

correctAnswer: Septic shock — gram-negative endotoxin (LPS) → cytokine storm (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) → vasodilation + myocardial depression; first-line: IV fluids + norepinephrine
quizQuestion: The most common type of distributive shock in ICU patients, often caused by gram-negative bacteria, features a cytokine-mediated vasodilation producing warm skin and high cardiac output early, followed by myocardial depression. Name the shock type and its first-line vasopressor.
explanation: Septic shock: gram-negative LPS (and gram-positive cell wall components) activate TLR4 (and TLR2) on macrophages → TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6 → endothelial dysfunction, massive vasodilation (↓SVR), and later myocardial depression. Early ('warm') septic shock: ↑CO, ↓SVR, warm skin (hyperdynamic phase). Late ('cold') phase: myocardial depression, ↓CO. Diagnostic criteria: sepsis with persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors + lactate ≥ 2 mmol/L despite adequate resuscitation. First-line vasopressor: norepinephrine (maintains MAP ≥ 65 mmHg). Vasopressin (second-line) spares catecholamines. Steroids if refractory.
visualDescription: ICU monitor showing hypotension, tachycardia, and high CO (early septic shock); IV line with norepinephrine label; gram-negative rod bacteria icon with LPS molecule
